findings.phase_name: OWASP A01 — Broken Access Control / A04 — Insecure Design
findings.title: Open redirect via `next` parameter in /auth/callback
findings.description: The auth callback route concatenates a user-controlled `next` query parameter directly onto the origin: `NextResponse.redirect(`${origin}${next}`)`. The string is not validated to be a relative path. When `next` begins with `@` (e.g. `?next=@evil.com`), the resulting URL `https://chadna.com@evil.com` is parsed by the URL spec as scheme=https, userinfo=`chadna.com`, host=`evil.com`. Modern browsers follow this redirect to evil.com. Active verification: `Response.redirect('https://chadna.com@evil.com', 302)` produces a Location header that parsers (and browsers) resolve to host=evil.com.
findings.exploit_scenario: 1) Attacker sends victim a phishing link `https://chadna.com/auth/callback?next=@attacker.com/login`. 2) Victim clicks — the domain looks legitimate. 3) The route handler skips the code-exchange branch (no `code` param), then calls `NextResponse.redirect('https://chadna.com@attacker.com/login')`. 4) Browser navigates to attacker.com/login. 5) Attacker shows a fake `chadna.com` login page (`session expired, please sign in`). 6) Victim re-enters credentials on attacker domain — captured. The attacker can now log into the victim's account and download passport scans, asylum evidence, etc.
findings.impact: Phishing facilitator. Particularly severe for an immigration-law app where a successful credential capture grants access to passports, I-94s, financial records, and persecution evidence. Open redirects on auth flows are a standard precursor to credential theft and have shipped in Supabase-pattern apps before.
findings.verification: verified — URL parser and Response.redirect tested in node showing host resolves to attacker domain

findings.title: RLS UPDATE policy on intake_messages allows clients to mutate ANY column (not just read_at)
findings.description: The policy `client marks own messages read` (intended per SECURITY.md and the comment in 0002_auth.sql to scope client UPDATE to marking read) has no column restriction. PostgreSQL RLS policies cannot scope UPDATE to specific columns — column-level access is controlled via GRANT, not RLS. Supabase by default grants UPDATE on all columns of public-schema tables to the `authenticated` role. The policy's `using` and `with check` clauses only verify intake ownership, not which columns changed. Result: a logged-in client can issue `UPDATE intake_messages SET body = ..., direction = ... WHERE id = ...` for any message on their own intake — including incoming messages from the attorney.
findings.exploit_scenario: 1) Client logs into the dashboard and notes the message-id of an incoming attorney message (visible because they own the intake). 2) Open browser dev tools, run: `(await import('@/lib/supabase/client')).getBrowserSupabase().from('intake_messages').update({body: 'I confirm the attorney told me the case is dismissed', direction: 'in'}).eq('id', '<msg-id>')`. 3) PostgREST issues UPDATE; RLS policy passes (client owns intake); columns update — including `body` of attorney messages. 4) The attorney's prior message now reads whatever the client typed. In an immigration legal-correspondence context, this enables fabricating attorney communications for legal disputes, regulatory complaints, or bar grievances.
findings.impact: Tampering with legal correspondence between attorney and client. Repudiation: clients can deny what the attorney sent or invent statements. For an attorney's malpractice exposure or a state-bar grievance, this is significant — the database is no longer trustworthy as a record of communication. Same risk applies to `direction` (clients could re-attribute their own messages as attorney messages or vice versa) and `subject`.
findings.recommendation:
Two layers, in order of preference:

1) Replace the broad UPDATE policy with column-scoped grants. Add to a new migration:

```sql
-- Revoke all UPDATE on intake_messages from authenticated, then
-- grant UPDATE only on the read_at column.
revoke update on public.intake_messages from authenticated;
grant update (read_at) on public.intake_messages to authenticated;
```

2) Tighten the RLS policy's `with check` to enforce that only `read_at` changed:

```sql
drop policy if exists "client marks own messages read" on public.intake_messages;
create policy "client marks own messages read" on public.intake_messages
  for update
  using (
    exists (select 1 from public.intakes
            where intakes.id = intake_messages.intake_id
              and intakes.client_user_id = auth.uid())
  )
  with check (
    -- updated row still owned by user
    exists (select 1 from public.intakes
            where intakes.id = intake_messages.intake_id
              and intakes.client_user_id = auth.uid())
  );
-- Then revoke + re-grant column-level UPDATE as in option 1.
```

GRANT-level column scoping is the durable fix; PostgreSQL enforces it before RLS even runs.
findings.playbook: After deploying the fix: (a) audit `audit_events` for any UPDATE-shaped activity from clients on `intake_messages`; (b) compare current message bodies against your Resend `sendClientMessage` and `sendAttorneyMessageAlert` email archive — emails are an out-of-band record of what was actually sent, so divergence flags tampering; (c) consider a content-addressed message-history table (insert-only, hash-chained) for legal-grade repudiation resistance.
findings.verification: verified — traced PostgREST + RLS evaluation path; confirmed no GRANT/REVOKE on column scope in any migration; default Supabase grant gives UPDATE on all columns to authenticated role

findings.title: No CSP / X-Frame-Options / HSTS / Referrer-Policy headers configured
findings.description: The Next.js config defines no `headers()` function and `proxy.ts` does not set security headers on responses. There's no Content-Security-Policy (mitigates XSS impact), no X-Frame-Options or `frame-ancestors` (mitigates clickjacking), no Strict-Transport-Security (forces HTTPS), no Referrer-Policy. Vercel sets a few defaults but not these. For an app handling immigration documents this is a meaningful hardening gap, especially when combined with Finding #1 (open redirect → phishing iframe is a viable stack).
findings.exploit_scenario: Without `frame-ancestors 'self'`, attacker.com iframes chadna.com/dashboard with `style=opacity:0` overlaid above their phishing form — when the user thinks they're clicking the phishing 'continue' button, they're actually clicking 'authorize' on the real dashboard (clickjacking). Without CSP, any future XSS finding goes from 'leak page contents' to 'exfiltrate session and PII' with no mitigation layer.
findings.impact: Defense-in-depth gap. Not directly exploitable today, but compounds with any future XSS or with Finding #1 to enable richer phishing.
findings.verification: self-verified — grepped next.config.ts and proxy.ts for header-setting code, none found

supply_chain_summary.notes: postcss <8.5.10 (CVSS 6.1, GHSA-qx2v-qp2m-jg93) is transitive via Next.js. Build-time only — no user-controlled CSS in this app — so not runtime exploitable. Below 8/10 daily-mode confidence gate. Upgrade Next.js when 16.4+ is available.

below_threshold_notes: Turnstile fail-open if TURNSTILE_SECRET_KEY env var is unset (operational misconfiguration, not current vuln)
Attorney message INSERT policy doesn't restrict direction — attorneys could insert direction='out' (insider trust issue)
requestIp() uses x-forwarded-for[0] and x-real-ip — Vercel-managed XFF is correct, but the code is platform-coupled if redeployed elsewhere
postcss XSS transitive (build-time only, not runtime exploitable)
Magic-byte sniff on PDF reads bytes[4] without explicit length check — JS returns undefined for out-of-bounds, so equality check fails safely (no bounds violation)

what_is_strong: SECURITY.md is real, not theater — accurately documents the threat model and tracks roadmap items
Stripe webhook signature verification is correct (raw body via req.text(), constructEvent, runtime=nodejs)
verifyStoredDocument re-validates path scope, traversal, MIME, size, AND magic bytes server-side after upload
Service-role admin client is properly walled off via 'server-only' import and only used in API routes
Audit logging on all sensitive actions (intake.submit, doc.download, intake.refund, message.send)
RLS is enabled on every public table; default-deny posture with explicit policies per role
Supabase Storage bucket private + service-role-only RLS + MIME whitelist + size cap baked into bucket config
Same-origin POST check + Turnstile + per-IP rate limit on intake/upload endpoints
Strong password policy (10+ chars, mixed case + digit) enforced both client and server-side
Auth callback uses exchangeCodeForSession (correct PKCE flow, not implicit)
Anonymous intakes auto-claim to user via on_auth_user_created trigger when emails match — secure by trigger, not by client trust
what_to_do_before_public_launch: Fix Findings #1 and #2 — these are HIGH
Add the security headers from Finding #3
Implement SECURITY.md roadmap items #2 (virus scan), #4 (per-doc encryption), #5 (MFA), #7 (rotating signed-URL TTL down to 5–15 min)
Get .gstack/ added to .gitignore so security reports stay local
